questions.question_text: When must a Montana seller provide the Property Condition Disclosure Statement to the buyer?
distractors: At the closing table itself
Within 10 days after acceptance
When the appraisal is complete
questions.explanation: Montana's seller disclosure law (MCA 70-20-502, enacted 2023) requires the seller's statement of known adverse material facts to be delivered prior to or contemporaneously with the execution of a contract — so providing it before the offer is accepted is the compliant practice. Unless the parties agree otherwise, the contract does not become effective until 3 days after the buyer receives the statement, and a buyer who receives it late may rescind within 3 days (MCA 70-20-504).

questions.question_text: A Montana seller's disclosure statement covers which areas of the property?
distractors: Only the roof and foundation areas
Only known environmental hazards
Only items visible during showings
questions.explanation: Montana's Property Condition Disclosure covers structure, plumbing, electrical, heating, water supply, septic systems, known defects, and environmental hazards. It is comprehensive and not limited to a single category.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent knows the basement floods but the seller's disclosure says no water issues. What must the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the known defect to the buyer
distractors: Rely solely on the seller's disclosure
Wait for the inspection report first
File a complaint with the Board first
questions.explanation: While agents do not guarantee the seller's disclosure, they cannot conceal known material defects. If the agent has personal knowledge of a defect, they must disclose it to the buyer regardless of what the seller's form says.

questions.question_text: Who bears primary responsibility for the accuracy of Montana's Property Condition Disclosure Statement?
distractors: The listing agent
The buyer's agent
The home inspector
questions.explanation: The seller is responsible for the accuracy of the Property Condition Disclosure Statement. The agent facilitates delivery but does not guarantee the contents. However, agents must not conceal defects they know about.

questions.question_text: A seller claims they are exempt from providing a property disclosure because the home is being sold as-is. Is this correct in Montana?
questions.correct_answer: No, as-is terms do not remove the duty
distractors: Yes, as-is sales are fully exempt
Yes, but only for homes sold without an agent
Only if the property is over 50 years old
questions.explanation: Selling as-is does not exempt a Montana seller from the disclosure statement required by MCA 70-20-502 (enacted 2023). The statute's actual exemptions (MCA 70-20-503) are things like court-ordered transfers, foreclosures, transfers between co-owners or close family — and a buyer's own waiver of the disclosure made in the contract at the time of submitting the offer. As-is language by itself is not on the list.

questions.question_text: A Montana agent wants to purchase a listing she represents. What must she disclose?
distractors: Only her real estate license number
Nothing if she pays the full price
Only the commission she would earn
questions.explanation: When a licensee has a personal interest in a transaction, such as purchasing a listed property, they must disclose that interest — acting in the dual capacity of broker and undisclosed principal is unprofessional conduct (MCA 37-51-321(1)(g)), and ARM 24.210.641(1)(g) requires a licensee seeking information from an owner to disclose their licensee status whether acting for their own account or for another. Documenting the disclosure in writing is best practice.

questions.question_text: Agent Martinez recommends a contractor who is her brother-in-law without telling the client about the relationship. What violation occurred?
distractors: An illegal referral fee arrangement
Unauthorized practice of contracting
Impermissible steering to a vendor
questions.explanation: Montana requires licensees to disclose any family relationship, financial relationship, or financial interest they have in a service provider they recommend (ARM 24.210.641(1)(e)). Failing to mention that the contractor is her brother-in-law is unprofessional conduct. The rule does not prescribe a format, but documenting the disclosure in writing is best practice.

questions.question_text: Which conflicts of interest must a Montana agent disclose?
distractors: Only direct financial interests
Only ownership in the property itself
Only relationships with other agents
questions.explanation: Montana requires disclosure of conflicts including a personal interest in the transaction (MCA 37-51-321(1)(g) makes acting as an undisclosed principal unprofessional conduct) and any family relationship, financial relationship, or financial interest in a recommended service provider (ARM 24.210.641(1)(e)). The rules do not prescribe a written format, though written disclosure is best practice.

questions.question_text: An agent owns 20% of a property management company and recommends it to her buyer client without mentioning her ownership. What has she done wrong?
questions.correct_answer: Failed to disclose her financial interest
distractors: Nothing, ownership under 50 percent is exempt
Nothing, the rule covers only attorneys and title firms
Nothing, the duty applies only to seller clients
questions.explanation: ARM 24.210.641(1)(e) makes it unprofessional conduct to recommend the services of a company without disclosing any family relationship, financial relationship, or financial interest the licensee has in it. The duty applies at any ownership percentage, to every category of recommended provider, and whether the person served is a principal or a third party. Documenting the disclosure in writing is best practice, though the rule does not prescribe a format.

questions.question_text: A listing agent discovers a crack in the foundation during a showing but does not tell prospective buyers. What duty was breached?
questions.correct_answer: Duty to disclose known material defects
distractors: Duty to order a professional inspection
Duty to repair defects before closing
Duty to guarantee structural soundness
questions.explanation: Montana agents must disclose known material defects to all parties in a transaction. Concealing a foundation crack discovered during a showing violates the agent's disclosure duty, regardless of who the agent represents.

questions.question_text: When must a Montana agent provide written agency disclosure to a prospective buyer?
distractors: When the purchase offer is signed
At the scheduled closing appointment
When financing has been approved
questions.explanation: Montana's statute sets fixed early trigger points: a buyer agent must give the written relationship disclosure at the time the buyer broker agreement is executed, and a statutory broker must give it at the time the broker first endeavors to locate property for the buyer (MCA 37-51-314(3), (4)). In every case the disclosure comes at the start of the relationship — waiting until the offer, financing, or closing is far too late.

questions.question_text: For a seller client, when must written agency disclosure be provided?
distractors: When the first offer is received
At the scheduled closing date
When the property is appraised
questions.explanation: Montana requires the initial relationship disclosure to be made to the seller at the time the listing agreement is executed (MCA 37-51-314(2)(a)) — which happens at the listing presentation when the seller signs. The distractors all come later in the transaction. Disclosures to the other side of the transaction are due when negotiations commence.

questions.question_text: A Montana agent switches from representing only the buyer to dual agency mid-transaction. What is required?
questions.correct_answer: New written consent from both parties
distractors: Verbal agreement from the buyer only
Notification to the Board within 5 days
Nothing if both parties are informed
questions.explanation: Any change in the agency relationship requires new written consent. Switching to dual agency requires informed written consent from both the buyer and seller before the agent can proceed in the new capacity.

questions.question_text: A buyer receives an agency disclosure form from an agent in Montana. What must the buyer provide in return?
distractors: A signed exclusive buyer agreement
A financial pre-qualification letter
Documented proof of Montana residency
questions.explanation: Montana requires written acknowledgment that the agency disclosure was received and understood. This creates a record that proper disclosure occurred at the appropriate time in the transaction.

questions.question_text: An agent fails to provide written agency disclosure at first substantive contact. What is the consequence?
questions.correct_answer: Potential disciplinary action by the Board
distractors: The transaction is automatically void
A mandatory fine of one thousand dollars
No consequence if disclosed later at closing
questions.explanation: Failing to provide written agency disclosure at first substantive contact violates Montana licensing law. The Board of Realty Regulation may impose disciplinary action including fines, license suspension, or revocation for this violation.
